Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For most recent month-end performance information, visit www.impaxam.com. Figures include reinvested dividends, capital gains distribution, and changes in principal value.

1First full month since inception. The inception date for Impax High Yield Bond Fund Institutional Class is 06/01/2004 and Investor Class is 10/08/1999, and Class A is 05/01/2013. As of 05/01/2024 prospectus, total annual Impax High Yield Bond Fund operating expenses, gross of any fee waivers or reimbursements (excluding Acquired Fund fees and expenses), for Institutional Class, Investors Class, and Class A shares are 0.68%, 0.93% and 0.93%, respectively.

The performance information shown for Institutional Class shares represents the performance of the Investor Class shares for the period prior to Institutional Class inception date (June 1, 2004). Expenses have not been adjusted to reflect the expenses allocable to Institutional Class shares. If such expenses were reflected,

the returns would be higher than those shown. Institutional Class shares’ average annual return since June 1, 2004 is 5.21% (annualized). The performance information shown for Class A represents the performance of the Investor Class shares for the period prior to Class A inception. Expenses have not been adjusted to reflect the expenses allocable to Class A shares. Class A inception date return since May 1, 2013 is 3.29% (annualized). A 1.00% CDSC (contingent deferred sales charge) may be charged on any shares sold within 18 months of purchase over $1 million. POP (public offering price) reflects the maximum sales load for the Fund’s Class A Shares of 4.50%.

Credit ratings are divided into categories ranging from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). The ratings shown are determined by using the middle rating of available Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organizations (NRSROs). When a rating from only two organizations is available, the lower rating is used. When a rating from only one is available, that rating is used. When a security is not rated by one of these organizations, it is designated as Not Rated. Securities designated as Not Rated do not necessarily indicate low credit quality. Holdings of the portfolio other than bonds are categorized as Other. Credit ratings apply to the underlying holdings of the portfolio and not the portfolio itself, are subjective opinions of NRSRO, may become stale and are subject to change.

Performance

• The Impax High Yield Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark on over the first quarter. Although security selection detracted, this was offset by positive allocation decisions.

Market review

• During the first quarter of 2025, the high yield bond market experienced significant volatility. Investors were initially bullish on the economy with a moderately strong risk appetite. However, the optimism surrounding US President Donald Trump’s tax cuts and deregulation proposals soon gave way to concerns over his sweeping tariffs and their impacts on growth and inflation. Against this backdrop, the strategy’s benchmark, the ICE BofA BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Constrained Index, was positive the quarter. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) met twice during the period and held rates steady at each meeting.

However, the Fed’s March Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) statement expressed increased uncertainty in the economic outlook. Although the updated ‘dot plot’ suggested the Fed would stay the course through 2025 and 2026 with just two 25 basis point (BPs) cuts in each of the years, the March Summary of Economic Projections revised 2025-2027 GDP growth downward while raising unemployment and core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) projections for 2025. US Treasury yields fell across the board on the increased uncertainty and prospects of a slowdown in economic growth with the 2-, 5-, and 10-year segments falling 36 bps, 43 bps, and 37 bps, respectively. The spread tightening seen early during the period quickly gave way to a steady widening after the mid-February release of the January FOMC meetings suggested the Fed remained concerned about inflation, the potential knock-on effects of looming tariffs, and uncertainty in the economic outlook. Towards the end of the period, spreads rapidly rose on increased tariff rhetoric from President Trump. The yield-to- worst on the benchmark rose by 13 bps while the par-weighted coupon fell modestly by 5 bps to end the period at 6.93% and 6.25%, respectively.

Market review

• During the period, performance across the high yield market was driven by the higher-quality segments, with BBs1 and Single Bs2 posting positive returns and outperforming the CCC and below3 segment which posted a negative return. From a sector perspective, the Banking, Real Estate, and Insurance sectors were the top performers. Conversely, the Transportation, Technology & Electronics, and Services sectors were the worst performers over the quarter.

• The par-weighted high yield bond default rate4, including distressed exchanges, continued to decrease to multi-year lows and well below the 25-year average. Leveraged loan default rates also decreased over the quarter. The decreases in both rates were driven by the lightest quarterly distressed exchange volume since the fourth quarter of 2022.

• The US$87.4bn of investor demand, including US$7.6bn of mutual fund inflows, matched the available supply which was split across US$68.3bn in gross new issuance and US$19.1bn in fallen angels5.

Contributors

• During the period, the overweight allocations to the Real Estate, Insurance, and Banking sectors positively contributed to performance. Security selection among the Real Estate, Telecommunications, and Capital Goods sectors also benefitted performance. Notable companies within these sectors include Medical Properties Trust (MPW), Regal Rexnord (RRX), and Viasat (VSAT). Regal Rexnord’s bonds generally rose throughout the quarter, despite lower-than-expected fourth quarter 2024 earnings. This occurred after management highlighted improving orders momentum relative to the prior year, as well as a negligible anticipated impact from tariffs. Viasat reported strong fourth quarter 2024 results and announced that the company had signed additional contracts with the US Department of Defense and several airlines which have calmed some of the fears of Starlink’s (STRLK) disruption of the industry. The bonds of Medical Properties Trust, were called at par.

Detractors

• The overweight allocations to the Technology & Electronics and Transportation sectors detracted over the quarter. The strategy’s lack of exposure to the Energy sector modestly detracted. Security selection within the Retail sector was the largest detractor to performance over the quarter. Security selection within the Automotive and Services sectors was also challenged but to a lesser extent. Notable companies within these sectors include Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA), Adient (ADNT), and Staples. The strategy did not own the Walgreens bonds when they traded up on the speculation that they might be redeemed after the company announced its leveraged buy-out by Sycamore. Staples’ bonds traded down due to the risk-off sentiment of the market driven by tariff news and general concerns about consumer sentiment. However, we maintain a strong conviction in this name over the long term. The bonds of Adient, an automobile parts manufacturer, similarly traded down on tariff concerns.

Outlook

• Credit spreads widened during the quarter, reflecting a significant rise in uncertainty across the global economy. Between unresolved tariff issues, weakening US economic data, and stalled progress between Ukraine and Russia, market participants have become more cautious. Lower-quality credit has underperformed so far this year, which is in line with expectations. Our investment team has reduced risk at the margins and remains ready to take further defensive steps if necessary.

• Although spreads have sold off sharply in recent weeks, they remain only modestly above their 10-year average and well below levels typically seen in past recessions. Compared to other recent periods of market anxiety – such as the growth concerns of October 2023 and the recession fears of 2022 – spreads are still relatively tight. Importantly, credit markets are not yet pricing in a recession.

• The outlook will largely depend on the outcome of tariff negotiations, companies’ ability to pass through higher costs, and the continued strength of the US labour market. If tariffs are resolved successfully, corporate fundamentals could remain stable, and a relief rally may follow given the current attractive yields. On the other hand, a breakdown in negotiations would sharply raise recession risks, likely resulting in wider spreads, higher yields, and increased defaults. For now, recession concerns remain lower than they were in 2023.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
